full_response:
We want to evaluate the single–index contraction  

  S_{jkmn} ≡ ε_{ijk}\,ε^{imn}.         (1)

Because we are in ordinary 3-dimensional Euclidean space the metric is just δ_{ij}, so raising or lowering an index does not change a Levi-Civita symbol: ε^{ijk}=ε_{ijk}.  

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Symmetries of S_{jkmn}
--------------------------------------------------------------------
•  ε_{ijk} is totally antisymmetric ⇒ ε_{ijk} = –ε_{ikj}.  
 Therefore S_{jkmn} is antisymmetric under j ↔ k.

•  The second ε–symbol is antisymmetric under m ↔ n, so S_{jkmn}
 is also antisymmetric under m ↔ n.

Thus S_{jkmn} must be built out of products of two Kronecker deltas
that are antisymmetric under those same exchanges.  The only tensor
with those symmetries and the correct index structure is

  A_{jkmn}=C(δ^{m}_{j}δ^{n}_{k}-δ^{n}_{j}δ^{m}_{k}),       (2)

with C a numerical constant still to be determined.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Determining the constant C
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Pick a simple set of indices that gives a non–zero value.
Choose (j,k,m,n)=(1,2,1,2).  Then

Left–hand side:    
S_{1212}=ε_{i12}ε^{i12}=ε_{312}ε^{312}= (+1)(+1)=+1.       (3)

Right–hand side using (2):  
A_{1212}=C(δ^{1}_{1}δ^{2}_{2}-δ^{2}_{1}δ^{1}_{2})=C(1·1-0·0)=C.   (4)

Equating (3) and (4) gives C=1.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Final result
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Hence

  ε_{ijk}\,ε^{imn}=δ^{m}_{j}δ^{n}_{k}-δ^{n}_{j}δ^{m}_{k}.       (5)

One can verify explicitly that both sides vanish whenever any pair of
indices coincide, and that they have identical antisymmetry properties,
so the equality holds for all j,k,m,n.

\boxed{\displaystyle \epsilon_{ijk}\,\epsilon^{imn}= \delta^{m}_{j}\delta^{n}_{k}-\delta^{n}_{j}\delta^{m}_{k}}

Confidence: 97%
